profile.name: Delbin
profile.availability: Open to work — full-time, part-time, contract (3–6 months) & freelance
profile.bio: I'm Delbin (Toe Htet Arkar), a Product Designer with 4 years of experience turning complex product ideas into intuitive user interfaces. I specialize in AI design and enterprise solutions, and I've worked across synthetic data & computer vision, defense training systems, healthcare, and edtech — usually owning the work from first research through final UI. Bilingual (EN / MM), based in Bangkok with remote experience across Singapore, Indonesia, California, and Myanmar.

caseStudies.title: The math wasn't the problem.
caseStudies.blurb: Grade 4 students kept stalling on Olympiad questions they could actually solve. Unscripted observation found three blockers — language, attention, navigation. None were math.

detail.subtitle: Everything around it was.
detail.summary: Grade 4 students kept stalling on Olympiad questions they could actually solve. We ran unscripted observations on a live competitor platform and found three blockers — language, attention, and navigation. None of them were about math. That finding reshaped every design decision that followed — including killing a visual theme the client already loved.

items.label: Team
items.value: Split team — I owned the student experience

items.label: Users observed
items.sub: 5 Grade 4 students and 3 guardians, on a live competitor platform

items.label: Modules tested
items.sub: Challenge, Training, Class — same problems in all three

items.label: Visual directions
items.sub: Built two full themes. Killed the one the client liked.

sections.title: Kids weren't failing the math. They were failing the interface.
paragraphs: We watched five students use a competitor platform. Every module told the same story: language got in the way, long videos lost them, and navigation was built for adults. When we translated the questions, they performed well. The barrier was the wrapper, not the work.

items.label: Problem
items.text: Kids 6–13 were disengaging from Olympiad math. Nobody could say why with evidence.

items.label: Method
items.text: Remote unscripted observation of 5 students + 3 guardians on KooBits. Scored on behaviour, time, and confidence.

items.label: Decision
items.text: Design for behaviour, not age. Strip language friction, shorten everything, separate learning from play.

items.label: Surfaces
items.text: Three modules rebuilt around familiar metaphors: a staircase, a book, a noticeboard.

items.label: Outcome
items.text: A calmer, clearer student app — and a theme pivot that saved the product from its own polish.

items.label: Lesson
items.text: A design that wins the room can still lose the child. Test the theme, not just the flow.

sections.title: Smart kids, stuck.
sections.subtitle: The brief was simple. The kids weren't.
paragraphs: An EdTech client wanted to bring Olympiad-level math to Myanmar's 6-to-13s — a local KooBits, but playful and built for children who'd never used an interface like it. The hard part was never the curriculum. It was that nobody could explain, with evidence, why children stalled. Was it the math? The reading? The screen itself?

sections.text: I didn't want to design for a six-year-old in theory. I wanted to watch one actually get stuck.

sections.title: Five students. Three modules. One method.
sections.subtitle: Before drawing a single screen, we watched real kids fail in real time.
paragraphs: We ran remote, unscripted observations with five Grade 4 students and three guardians, using KooBits as a live stand-in. Unscripted on purpose — the moment you give a child a script, you stop seeing how they actually behave. We tested three modules: Challenge, Training, and Class, scoring every session through three lenses.

notes.title: Usage behaviour
notes.text: Could they use the interface without help? How much cognitive load did it cost?

notes.title: Confidence
notes.text: Rated "Not Much" across all three modules.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: Child testing the competitor platform ]

sections.text: Every module told the same story in a different accent.

sections.title: Three blockers. None of them were math.
sections.subhead: The same problems showed up in Challenge, Training, and Class.

items.title: Language barrier
items.text: Students struggled to translate questions. But when we translated for them, they performed well. The math wasn't hard — the English was.

items.title: Attention
items.text: Long tutorial videos lost them every time. Most never watched the solution explanations. If it wasn't short, it didn't exist.

items.title: Navigation + dependence
items.text: Students needed guardian help for basic tasks. Navigation was built for adults — kids couldn't find their way through alone.

sections.title: Teachers hit the same walls.
paragraphs: We also got teacher feedback on the existing platform: onboarding screens weren't clear, reports were confusing, and one teacher couldn't find the quiz after finishing a tutorial.

sections.text: When the questions were translated, they could do the math. The interface was the barrier, not the content.

sections.title: Two apps. I owned one.
sections.subtitle: With the problem defined, the team split.
paragraphs: A parallel group took the parent app — progress tracking, subscriptions, oversight. I took the student experience. I mapped how a child actually moves through the platform — log in, pick a class, watch a short video, take a quiz — and built a user flow and information architecture simple enough for a six-year-old to hold in their head.
The research gave us clear rules: strip language friction, keep everything short, and separate learning content from gamified content so neither dilutes the other.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: User flow ]
[ IMAGE: Information architecture ]

sections.title: Three modules, three metaphors.
sections.subtitle: Low-fi first — because the cheapest place to be wrong is a wireframe.
paragraphs: Wireframes came before anything polished. They surfaced three rules fast: navigation needed large, tappable targets; learning and play had to be visibly separated; and mobile was its own design problem — not a shrunk desktop.
Each module then borrowed a metaphor kids already understood.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: Low-fi wireframes — desktop ]
[ IMAGE: Low-fi wireframes — mobile ]

items.title: Challenge — a staircase.
items.text: Progress you can see. Completed steps, locked steps, and retries laid out as a climb. Practice feels like going up, not in circles.

items.title: Class — a book.
items.text: Linear and calm. Short video, then a quiz, then the next. Content broken into pieces small enough to survive a short attention span.

items.title: Training — a noticeboard.
items.text: HOTS and Singapore Math, organised by proficiency and high scores. Chalkboard styling that feels warm, not clinical.

sections.title: The theme everyone loved was the wrong one.
sections.subtitle: We built a beautiful direction, then killed it.
paragraphs: The first visual concept was a space-exploration theme — cosmic visuals, glowing gradients, futuristic UI. The client liked it immediately. Then we built it out, and the truth surfaced: too complex, and it pulled against the core promise of a calm, pressure-free place to learn.

So we pivoted to a second direction — cleaner, brighter, friendlier. Less spectacle, more clarity. It was the harder call because the first version was likeable. But "likeable in a review" and "right for the user" aren't the same thing.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: Space theme — initial version ]
[ IMAGE: Final version ]

sections.text: The space theme won the room. It would have lost the child.

sections.title: What this project taught me.

items.title: Design for behaviour, not age.
items.text: "Six-to-thirteen" isn't a spec. Short attention, limited reading, and a need for visual clarity are the real constraints — and they only surface when you watch real children.

items.title: The barrier is usually the wrapper.
items.text: The kids could do the math. Language, video length, and navigation were doing the failing. Fixing the frame beat simplifying the content.

items.title: Align stakeholders before high-fi.
items.text: Sharing research, flows, and wireframes before polished screens meant the theme pivot was a course-correction, not a crisis.

items.title: A loved design can still be wrong.
items.text: The space theme proved client enthusiasm is a signal, not a verdict. The user is the verdict.

sections.title: If I could go back.
paragraphs: I'd test the visual theme the same way we tested the flow — cheaply, with real kids, before building it out fully. We caught the navigation issues through observation but let the space theme run all the way to a finished design before discovering it didn't fit. A rough themed prototype in front of five children would have caught that in an afternoon, not a sprint.

caseStudies.paletteLabel: The cameras were watching. Nobody was.
caseStudies.title: The cameras were watching.
caseStudies.blurb: A full-stack Vision AI Operations platform for defence and security operators — validate a model against real footage before it ever watches a live camera, then monitor, triage, and turn detections into audit-ready incident reports. I designed the full MVP end-to-end.
caseStudies.mockLabel: [ Accel — Dashboard, the central ops view ]

detail.summary: Computer vision teams lose accuracy, time, and operator trust in the gap between a model that scores well in benchmarks and one that holds up in the field. Accel is Sigmawave AI's Vision AI Operations platform — it closes that gap with one loop: validate a model against real footage before deployment, watch it live once deployed, and turn every detection into an audit-ready incident. I designed the full MVP platform end-to-end.
detail.subtitle: Complete operator autonomy, from validation to live monitoring.

items.label: Platform sections
items.sub: Monitor, Manage, Deploy, System, and Profile Settings — the full operator lifecycle

items.label: Modules designed
items.sub: From Live Monitoring and Detection Feed to Run Analysis, Rules Library, and Model Management

items.label: Persistent problems solved
items.sub: The validation gap, tooling fragmentation, and detection logic locked behind engineering

sections.title: Operators don't fail to respond. They fail to see it in time.
paragraphs: Accel exists because most computer vision programs lose their edge after the demo. A model that scores well in benchmark testing fails in the field — different lighting, different angles, occlusion nobody tested for — and the failure only shows up after deployment, when it's expensive to fix. Teams also stitch together separate tools for datasets, testing, rules, and live monitoring, and the seams between them hide the root cause. Accel is a single platform that closes that loop: validate a model before it goes live, watch it once it's live, and turn every detection into something an operator can act on.

items.label: Problem
items.text: Models that pass benchmarks fail in the field, tools don't talk to each other, and defining detection logic requires an engineer — locking out the operators who understand the job best.

items.label: Method
items.text: Mapped the full operator lifecycle from the PRD — validation, live monitoring, incident response — then designed each of the platform's 17 modules against real operator workflows.

items.label: Decision
items.text: Give Run Analysis — pre-deployment validation against real footage — the design investment of a hero feature, not a settings screen.

items.label: Surfaces
items.text: Monitor (dashboard, live monitoring, recordings, incident reports), Manage (sites, cameras, NVRs, models, rules), Deploy (run analysis, deploy models), System (users, device health, config, logs).

items.label: Outcome
items.text: A platform built for defence and security operators — audit-trail grade for customers like DSTA, MINDEF, and IMDA — where a model earns trust before it ever watches a live feed.

items.label: Lesson
items.text: The validation report is the deliverable users actually came for, not the dashboard around it.

sections.title: Three problems, one operator.
sections.subtitle: Validation gap, tooling fragmentation, and detection logic locked behind engineering.
paragraphs: Every computer vision team we spoke to described some version of the same three problems.

notes.title: Validation gap
notes.text: Models that score well in benchmark testing fail in real deployment conditions because of lighting, angle, occlusion, and environmental variation. These failures typically surface only after deployment, when remediation is expensive.

notes.title: Tooling fragmentation
notes.text: Teams stitch together separate platforms for dataset management, video-based testing, performance visualisation, rule definition, deployment, and live monitoring. The seams between these tools obscure root cause analysis and slow iteration.

notes.title: Detection-logic accessibility
notes.text: Defining when a model should trigger an alert typically requires engineering involvement, locking out the operations and domain experts who understand the use case best.

sections.text: A model that scores well in a benchmark and a model an operator trusts in production are not the same thing. Accel's job is to close that gap before it becomes an incident.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: Accel — dashboard, the central ops view ]

sections.title: Pre-deployment validation is the differentiator.
sections.subtitle: Test a model against real footage before it ever watches a live camera.
paragraphs: Run Analysis is the MVP's core moment. A user selects a model, picks a preferred SOP VLM for reasoning, and uploads representative footage. The system produces a score, a structured activity log, and a VLM reasoning narrative that describes and reasons about what it saw — all exportable as a report.

A Past History view lets users search previous analyses by keyword or date, and open any one to see the full report again. It's the traceable evidence an operator needs before they'll trust a model with a live feed.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: Run Analysis — score, activity log, VLM reasoning ]

sections.title: What makes a validation trustworthy.

items.title: The score
items.text: A single number an operator can hold a model to — the pass/fail bar before it ever reaches a camera.

items.title: The activity log
items.text: A structured, step-by-step record of what the model flagged during the test run, not just a final verdict.

items.title: The VLM reasoning narrative
items.text: Plain-language reasoning about the footage — why the model flagged what it flagged, in words an operator (not an engineer) can evaluate.

sections.text: Run Analysis deserved design investment proportional to its strategic importance — it's the moment an operator decides whether to trust the model at all.

sections.title: Once it's live, the feed has to earn attention every shift.
sections.subtitle: Dashboard, Live Monitoring, Recordings, and the Detection Feed.
paragraphs: The Dashboard is the at-a-glance operations screen — a persistent time filter (today, 7 days, 30 days, or custom) applies across every panel, with drill-down navigation into the module underneath. Live Monitoring gives operators a configurable multi-camera grid with a real-time health indicator per tile and active/inactive counters.

The Detection Feed is the structured log underneath it all: filterable by date range, site, area, severity, camera, and model, sorted newest or oldest. Every card surfaces the detection title, severity, case number, model used, precision and confidence scores, with one-glance actions to raise it as an official incident, dismiss it, or attach it to an existing report.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: Detection Feed — filterable list, severity + confidence ]

sections.title: From site to camera to a model that's live.
sections.subtitle: Site Management, Cameras, NVRs, Rules Library, Model Management, and Deploy Models.
paragraphs: Site Management defines the physical hierarchy — sites, areas, and lanes — that drives zone options everywhere else in the platform. Cameras get added with RTSP stream details, recording configuration, and boundary-box zone drawing, then linked to an NVR channel. NVR Devices track storage health and retention, with age-based or channel-based cleanup.

Rules Library is where detection logic becomes something a non-engineer can own: pre-built templates for common scenarios (perimeter intrusion, PPE compliance, crowd density), and every rule gets an auto-generated plain-English summary. Model Management assembles an SOP-compliant sequence of processing steps and attaches rules from the library — the artefact that gets tested in Run Analysis and, once validated, pushed live in Deploy Models to a specific site, area, and camera.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: Rules Library — rule card with plain-English summary ]

sections.text: The plain-English rule summary is the operator's anchor for confidence. It has to stay prominent and accurate — that was a non-negotiable design constraint.

sections.title: A detection is a flag. A report is the record.
sections.subtitle: Auto-generated from high-severity rules, or built manually from a recording.
paragraphs: High-severity rule violations auto-generate an Incident Report; operators can also build one manually — locate the footage by site, area, camera, date, and detection type, populate the metadata (title, severity, assigned personnel, status, description), and save. The system links the report to the exact footage timestamp and produces an audit-ready record.

Counters (Total, Pending, Acknowledged, Resolved) sit above a filterable list and a detail view with the full audit trail, linked evidence, and an exportable PDF — distinct from the Activity Log, which is append-only and system-wide rather than case-specific.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: Incident Reports — counters, list, and detail view ]

sections.title: Governance underneath the operator's view.
sections.subtitle: User Management, Device Health, System Configuration, Activity Logs.
paragraphs: Owners and Admins manage the org's membership and role-scoped permissions in User Management, watch every camera and NVR's online/offline status and disk usage in Device Health, and tune alert thresholds and refresh cadence in System Configuration. Every state-changing action — unlinking an NVR, deleting a camera, editing a rule — writes to an append-only, audit-grade Activity Log, the layer that matters most to public-sector buyers who never see a demo screen.

sections.title: What this project taught me.

items.title: Design the validation moment like a hero feature.
items.text: Run Analysis carries the platform's entire value proposition. It earned more design time than any settings screen, because it's the moment trust actually gets built.

items.title: Trust is built before deployment, not during it.
items.text: Closing the validation gap upfront changes how an operator relates to every alert that comes after. Skimping on Run Analysis would have undermined every other module.

items.title: Plain language is a trust feature, not a nice-to-have.
items.text: Auto-generated rule summaries and consistent severity colour language exist so operators — not just engineers — can act on a detection with confidence.

items.title: Governance has to be invisible until it's needed.
items.text: Device health, activity logs, and audit trails matter most to public-sector buyers who evaluate the platform on paper before they ever click through it.

sections.title: If I could go back.
paragraphs: I'd get real operators into Run Analysis earlier. The per-rule breakdown and VLM reasoning were designed against the PRD's happy journey, not a live triage shift — one session watching an operator validate a model under time pressure would have surfaced friction faster than another round of my own iteration.

And I'd lock the severity colour language across Detection Feed, Incident Reports, and System Configuration before designing any of them individually. Retrofitting one consistent palette across five modules cost more than defining it up front would have.

caseStudies.paletteLabel: A working product. A better one to build.
caseStudies.title: A working product. A better one to build.
caseStudies.blurb: Terra generates synthetic data to train AI vision models — datasets that are dangerous, expensive, or impossible to collect in the real world. A working version already existed. My job was to redesign it end-to-end: better usability, more features, and a real fix for the pain points users hit in the original.

detail.summary: Terra generates synthetic data to train AI vision models — the datasets that are dangerous, expensive, or impossible to collect in the real world. A working version already existed. My job was to redesign it: better usability, more features, and a real fix for the pain points users hit in the original. I owned it end-to-end, from competitor research to the final 3D builder.
detail.subtitle: Redesigning Terra's synthetic data platform — from research to a full 3D builder.

items.label: Workflows supported
items.sub: Visual Synthetic Data (VSD) full 3D, and Hybrid Synthetic Data (HSD) synthetic logic on real video

items.label: Feature modules
items.sub: Dashboard, org management, 3D editor, object and environment settings, timeline, generation

items.label: Export formats
items.sub: Datasets auto-annotated in COCO, YOLO, and Pascal VOC — train-ready on export

sections.title: The tool worked. It just needed to be better.
paragraphs: Terra wasn't new. A working version already shipped — but it was rigid. Scenes were hard to customise, there was no real camera control, lighting was fixed, and teams couldn't scale their work. I redesigned the whole thing: benchmarked the leading synthetic data tools, learned Blender and Unreal so I actually understood the 3D work our users did, gathered feedback from real users of the old version, and rebuilt Terra around a focused, canvas-first 3D builder.

items.label: Problem
items.text: The original Terra worked, but it was rigid: limited scene customisation, no camera control, fixed lighting, poor scalability for teams.

items.label: Method
items.text: Benchmarked competitors, took a month of Blender and Unreal training, gathered feedback from existing users, then rebuilt the flow.

items.label: Decision
items.text: Redesign around a canvas-first 3D builder where users interact with scenes in real time — not a rigid, disconnected editor.

items.label: Surfaces
items.text: Dashboard, org management, 3D scene editor, object and environment settings, timeline, generation and export.

items.label: Outcome
items.text: A platform that turns scene-building into real, train-ready datasets — auto-annotated and ready to drop straight into a model pipeline.

items.label: Lesson
items.text: The best design comes from staying close to the real work users are trying to do.

sections.title: It worked. It was just rigid.
sections.subtitle: A working product that users couldn't move freely inside.
paragraphs: Terra already had a working version when I joined the project. The technology was sound — it generated synthetic data. But using it was the problem. Scene customisation was limited. There was no way to move the camera, no precise object placement, no way to preview how a scene would look under different lighting. Editing felt disconnected, and the platform didn't scale for teams.
My role as Product Owner and Designer was to redesign it — better usability, more features, and a real fix for the pain points users faced in the original.

sections.text: The product wasn't broken. The experience was. My job was to redesign the experience without breaking what worked.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: Terra old version — the original interface ]

sections.title: I didn't design first. I learned first.
sections.subtitle: Benchmarks, then a month inside Blender and Unreal.
paragraphs: To know what to design, I studied the field. I benchmarked the leading synthetic data platforms — Synthesis AI, Anyverse, Bifrost AI, Parallel Domain — to understand where the industry bar sat and where Terra could go further.

But Terra runs on 3D workflows, and I couldn't design for something I didn't understand. So I took a month of Blender and Unreal Engine training with top creators — scene design, materials, lighting, animation. That month changed how I designed. I stopped guessing at what a 3D artist needed and started designing from how the work actually gets done.

sections.text: You can't design a 3D tool from the outside. I learned the craft first, then designed for it.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: Benchmark board — competitor tools compared ]

sections.title: What the new Terra had to solve.
sections.subtitle: Straight from the people who used the old one.
paragraphs: I reached out to users of the previous version and gathered direct feedback. The same problems kept surfacing: limited scene customisation, unintuitive tools, minimal control over rendering, and poor scalability for teams.

I spent the following weeks turning that into direction — pulling best practices from the platforms and creative tools I'd studied, and building a new user flow that reflected the expanded feature set and fixed the old limitations. This phase wasn't just about refining the experience. It was about expanding what the product could be.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: New user flow — the redesigned journey ]

sections.title: Getting everyone to see it before building it.
sections.subtitle: Mid-fidelity proposal, early feedback, aligned direction.
paragraphs: Before touching high-fidelity design, I brought the research and early concepts to stakeholders. I walked them through the proposed user flows, showed how each feature answered a specific pain point from the old platform, and laid out the key improvements planned for the new version.

To make the direction tangible, I prepared a mid-fidelity proposal showing how the updated layout would look and work. It let stakeholders see the redesign clearly and give feedback early — before it was expensive to change.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: Mid-fidelity proposal shown to stakeholders ]

sections.title: Same product. A frame that finally fits.
sections.subtitle: The redesign, told through the moments that define it.
paragraphs: With research, layout direction, and the revised flow aligned, I moved into high-fidelity design. Here are the moments that define how the new Terra works.

sections.title: Users used to lose track of their own work.
paragraphs: The old dashboard had no visual hierarchy and made resuming or managing multiple projects a chore. I rebuilt it around a visually rich project overview — recently opened projects shown as large, recognisable thumbnails with key metadata like last edit date and owner, so users can pick up where they left off and collaborate fast.

Navigation was the other pain point. Jumping between projects or finding admin tools felt clunky, so I restructured the sidebar and tab system for seamless workspace switching. Tools like project settings and org management sit contextually — there when you need them, out of the way when you don't.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: Terra redesigned dashboard — project thumbnails, restructured sidebar ]

sections.title: No camera control. No precise placement. No lighting preview.
paragraphs: The old Builder was missing the basics users expected. Editing felt rigid and disconnected — hard to build anything dynamic or realistic. I reimagined the whole thing around a focused, canvas-based interface where users interact with their scenes in real time. Two panels carry most of the weight: Object Settings and Environment Settings.

sections.caption: Object Settings panel
sections.label: [ IMAGE: Object Settings panel ]

sections.caption: Environment Settings panel
sections.label: [ IMAGE: Environment Settings panel ]

sections.title: Two features define where the product is heading.
paragraphs: The redesign also brought in AI features I designed end-to-end. A third, Auto Segment Anything (ASA), handles automatic image segmentation and occlusion between real images and 3D objects — designed, but not covered in depth here.

sections.title: Two AI features, designed end-to-end.

items.title: Restyle (MAT)
items.text: Synthetic data often looks synthetic. MAT lets users upload a real-world reference image, and Terra extracts its visual characteristics — lighting, colour grading, noise, lens artifacts — and applies them to the synthetic output. Users control the strength and preview before exporting. The goal: datasets close enough to real that models actually generalise.

items.title: Sigma Agentic Builder (SAB)
items.text: AI-assisted scene building. Instead of placing every object by hand, users describe what they want and the system helps assemble it.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: MAT interface — upload reference, preview adaptation ]
[ IMAGE: SAB interface — if available ]

sections.title: From scene to train-ready data.
sections.subtitle: The whole point of Terra: usable ML data, not just pretty renders.
paragraphs: The generation flow ties it together. A user builds a scene, hits Generate, picks image or video, and the system processes the job in the background. A Work Orders panel tracks every job — in progress, completed, failed — so users can queue multiple generations and come back when they're ready.

What comes out is the payoff: datasets auto-annotated in COCO, YOLO, or Pascal VOC — labeled, structured, and ready to train on with no manual annotation step. That's the difference between a 3D render and a training asset.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: Generate modal — image / video, generation settings ]
[ IMAGE: Work Orders panel — job tracking, download states ]
[ IMAGE: Received dataset — generated images with annotations/labels ]

sections.title: I took the dataset straight into Roboflow.
paragraphs: To prove the loop closes, I took a batch of Terra-generated images straight into Roboflow and trained a model on them — no real-world photos in the set. The dataset dropped in cleanly, annotations intact, and trained without manual cleanup. That's the whole thesis of the product in one test: build a scene, generate, train.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: Roboflow — Terra dataset uploaded, training run on generated images ]

sections.text: A synthetic data platform is only as good as what a model can learn from it. The dataset going straight into Roboflow and training clean was the real proof.

sections.title: What this project taught me.

items.title: Learn the craft before you design for it.
items.text: A month in Blender and Unreal changed every decision that followed. You can't design a 3D tool well from the outside — I had to understand how scenes are actually built, lit, and rendered first.

items.title: The best direction comes from the old product's users.
items.text: The people who struggled with the original version told me exactly what to fix. Listening to the real work beat any assumption I could have made in Figma.

items.title: The dataset is the deliverable, not the scene.
items.text: It's easy to over-invest in how the editor looks and forget users come for train-ready data. Designing generation and export to end in clean, labeled output mattered as much as any editor panel.

items.title: Ownership means deciding what matters.
items.text: As Product Owner I defined the MVP, prioritised features, and shaped the vision from the ground up. That freedom only works if you stay disciplined about what actually earns its place.

sections.title: If I could go back.
paragraphs: I'd get real users into the redesigned Builder sooner. The research and stakeholder alignment were solid, but the canvas-first flow was something I mostly validated internally and in Figma. One session watching an engineer try to build a complex scene would have surfaced friction faster than another round of my own iteration.

And I'd document the design system earlier. With 35+ modules across a dashboard and a full 3D editor, the patterns I set up front held the redesign together — but I built a lot of it reactively. Formalising it sooner would have saved rework as the feature set expanded.

caseStudies.paletteLabel: Selling the art was never the hard part.
caseStudies.title: Selling the art was never the hard part.
caseStudies.blurb: Myanmar had artists with nowhere to sell and buyers with no reason to feel safe. 50+ interviews reframed the brief: Kanote wasn't an art shop — it was the first trust layer for a market that never had one.

detail.subtitle: Earning the trust was.
detail.summary: Myanmar had artists with nowhere to sell and buyers with no reason to feel safe. Fifty-plus interviews and gallery visits reframed the brief: Kanote wasn't an art shop — it was the first trust layer for a market that had never had one.

items.label: Voices in research
items.sub: Artists and art lovers interviewed 1:1, plus gallery owners and curators

items.label: Usability testers
items.sub: Whose feedback forced us to cut features, not add them

items.label: Core solutions
items.sub: Protected payments, a community feed, and a story behind every piece

sections.title: We set out to build a store. The research said build trust.
paragraphs: In a market unfamiliar with digital art platforms, the blocker was never listing artwork — it was confidence. Artists feared being unseen and underpaid; buyers feared paying for something they couldn't hold. Every core decision became an answer to one question: why should I trust this?

items.label: Problem
items.text: Myanmar artists had talent but no trusted platform; buyers had no safe, transparent way to purchase.

items.label: Method
items.text: 50+ one-on-one interviews, gallery visits with owners and curators, then a survey to verify the patterns at scale.

items.label: Decision
items.text: Design for trust and visibility first. Commerce follows confidence, not the other way around.

items.label: Surfaces
items.text: Protected payment paths, a community feed for discovery, and an artist-story panel on every artwork.

items.label: Outcome
items.text: A simpler, calmer platform — after usability testing made us cut the features we were proudest of.

items.label: Lesson
items.text: When users ask for more, the right answer is often less. Prioritisation is design.

sections.title: A market with no front door.
sections.subtitle: Plenty of art. Plenty of buyers. No trusted place for them to meet.
paragraphs: Myanmar's emerging artists had work to sell and no real platform for it — and buyers had no transparent, safe way to pay for something they couldn't physically inspect. The market had barely encountered a digital art platform, so we couldn't lean on habits that didn't exist. Building a storefront would have been easy. Building one people trusted enough to use was the real problem.

sections.text: The hard part wasn't listing artwork. It was giving two strangers a reason to believe the transaction.

sections.title: Fifty conversations and a gallery tour.
sections.subtitle: We went to where the art already lived — online and off.
paragraphs: We spoke with more than fifty artists and art lovers one-on-one, through social media, community networks, and in-person meetups. Then we left the screen — the team visited local galleries and sat with owners and curators who showed us how emerging artists really navigate the scene. Interviews gave depth; a structured survey across Myanmar verified the patterns at scale.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: Research team visiting galleries & exhibitions ]
[ IMAGE: Survey responses ]

sections.text: Qualitative depth first, then quantitative proof. We refused to design on a vivid anecdote.

sections.title: Two users. One shared need.
sections.subtitle: Different goals, same blocker — trust.
paragraphs: The research resolved into two personas. The emerging artist needed visibility and fair, protected opportunities to sell. The passionate art lover needed a safe, transparent way to buy. Their goals diverged, but both pointed at the same missing ingredient: trust. From there we wrote sharp problem statements and translated them into user flows for browsing, uploading, buying on installments, and community.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: User personas ]
[ IMAGE: User flow — browse, upload, purchase, community ]

sections.title: Three answers to one question.
sections.subtitle: Every core feature was a reason to trust the platform.
paragraphs: We wireframed the four jobs that mattered — browse, buy, upload, connect — and iterated fast, killing anything unclear before it hardened. Then the visual language: bright and inviting, clean layouts, large touch targets, vibrant accents matching the creativity of the people using it. Three solutions did the heavy lifting, each answering the same question — why should I trust this?

sections.label: [ IMAGE: Low-fidelity wireframes ]

items.title: Two ways to pay — both protected.
items.text: Buyers choose Pay in Full or an installment plan, each governed by terms that protect artist and buyer alike. Flexibility for buyers; security for the artists selling.

items.title: A community, not a catalogue.
items.text: A feed where artists post work, share updates, and talk directly with collectors — turning a marketplace into a place where rising talent actually gets discovered.

items.title: Every artwork tells its story.
items.text: An About panel on each piece pairs the artist's story — background, inspiration, process — with hard specs like medium, materials, dimensions, and edition. Context plus facts, so buyers connect and commit.

sections.title: We built too much. Then we cut it.
sections.subtitle: Our proudest version was also our most overwhelming.
paragraphs: The first full build answered every request — too many features, too many paths, navigation that made first-time users freeze. We put the prototype in front of 15+ participants and watched the overwhelm in real time. Artists loved that Kanote handled delivery; buyers wanted clearer order tracking and richer profiles. So we did the harder thing than adding — we subtracted. Prioritisation simplified the layout, refined onboarding, and stripped friction from the core flows. The finished screens are calmer, and that calm is the result.

sections.label: [ IMAGE: Usability testing — 15+ participants ]
[ IMAGE: Before / after — the simplified version ]

sections.text: The first build answered every request. That was exactly what was wrong with it.

sections.title: What this project taught me.

items.title: Commerce plus community is the product
items.text: Myanmar's artists didn't need a shop. They needed somewhere to be seen, heard, and supported — and the community turned out to be as load-bearing as the checkout.

items.title: Design for the local context
items.text: A market new to digital art needed trust, simplicity, and cultural fluency built in. Patterns borrowed wholesale from mature markets would have quietly missed.

items.title: Reading qualitative data is a skill
items.text: Fifty interviews are noise until you structure them. Affinity mapping turned scattered feedback into the empathy that drove the design.

items.title: Prioritisation is design
items.text: The strongest version of Kanote came from what we removed, not what we added. When everyone wants more, choosing less is the job.

sections.title: If I could go back.
paragraphs: I'd test for overwhelm earlier. We caught the feature bloat in usability testing — the right place, but late. A stricter prioritisation pass before the first full build would have spared a version we had to dismantle. And I'd close the loop with numbers: task success on the simplified flows, drop-off before and after the onboarding refinement, and completed purchases by payment type.

moreProjects.title: The ops table that runs itself
moreProjects.blurb: A spreadsheet-grade table for non-technical teams — sorting, bulk edits, and audit trails baked in.

moreProjects.title: Unified design tokens
moreProjects.blurb: One token pipeline feeding Figma, web, and native — themes flip without a single hand-edit.

writing.title: Designing trust into autonomous interfaces
writing.excerpt: When software acts on its own, the UI's job is to make the seams legible.

writing.title: The design system is the product
writing.excerpt: Why tokens, not screens, are the real deliverable.

writing.title: Shipping is a design decision
writing.excerpt: A case for designers who hold the keyboard, not just the canvas.

bullets: Spent 1.5 year designing the end-to-end flow of IMT TRMS, a training resource management system for MINDEF Singapore
Shipped across defence, education, computer vision tooling, and physical security — four domains in one company
Promoted to Product Owner/Designer — redesigned both Terra (World Builder) and Accel (Model Validator) from scratch
Built and delivered the design system used across all products
Picked up computer vision along the way and started contributing to CV-related tasks across products
Designed the entire Pistil healthcare platform — user, admin, and doctor dashboards plus the mobile app
Started every feature in Low-Fi wireframes before touching Hi-Fi
Let prototypes do the talking in dev handoffs — less confusion, fewer surprises
Designed two products from the ground up — Global Caring, a nursing platform, and Sofinise, a crypto payment gateway
Worked directly with developers and stakeholders on both projects
Worked alongside senior UX designers from Myanmar on client projects
Main project was Jar Aye, an Olympiad math platform for young learners
Followed the full design workflow — research, wireframes, prototyping, testing, and handoff
First full-time design role. Mostly consumer products — blogging apps, ride-hailing, and other mini-apps
Tried to ship a product every month, which meant learning to move fast without cutting corners
Edit and publish video and podcast content for UXMM events across YouTube and Instagram
Manage the org's media library
Interviewed 50+ artists from local Myanmar art communities to understand their needs and pain points
Ran user research and built wireframes with a cross-functional team
Designed Kanote, a digital art marketplace for Myanmar artists
Built a blog website for the organisation from scratch
Learned layout fundamentals and design tooling from a senior web design instructor
Worked with other UI designers on interface designs
Got to see how different designers approach the same problem — picked up habits I still use

contributions.title: RIC Applied AI Innovation Challenge (AIIC 2026)
contributions.description: AI hackathon at Rangsit University. Built a working prototype in a team of four, pitched it live to judges. Won Second Runner-Up.

contributions.title: Falling Walls Lab 2023
contributions.description: 3-day programme on pitching research-backed ideas. My topic was Breaking the Wall of Art Community Barriers — an early version of what later became Kanote. Didn't make the finals, but it was where I first had to frame a design idea as a business case.

contributions.title: Techstars Startup Weekend Yangon 2023
contributions.description: 3-day startup challenge. Built the pitch deck for Kanote Art Platform and presented it to judges and investors.

contributions.title: Iterate UX Research Challenge
contributions.description: 6-week UXR project for CookieYes, a cookie consent platform. Ran the full research cycle and presented findings to a judging panel.

contributions.title: Iterate UX Design Challenge
contributions.description: 6-week UX design competition. Ideated, prototyped, and tested a solution as a team, then pitched it to judges.
